summary_quote: yaml.safe_load is used correctly; no secrets or auth surface present. One medium concern: the config loader silently accepts any YAML file path without validation, enabling arbitrary file reads if cfg_path ever becomes user-controlled.

findings.title: cfg_path accepted without validation; arbitrary file read if caller ever supplies user-controlled input
findings.explanation: load_config() calls path.read_text() on whatever Path is constructed from cfg_path with no validation that the path stays within the project directory. Currently train() is called only from __main__ with a hardcoded default, so the risk is low-to-none today. However, if a future caller passes cfg_path from a CLI argument, environment variable, or API request without sanitization, an attacker could supply an arbitrary path and the content would be parsed. Threat model — Asset: any readable file on the host filesystem; Threat: path traversal via unsanitized cfg_path; Impact: arbitrary file read; Likelihood: low now (single __main__ entrypoint), but easily promoted to high the moment a CLI flag or web surface is added.
findings.suggestion: Add a path allowlist check before reading: resolve the path and assert it shares the expected config root, e.g. `resolved = Path(path).resolve(); assert resolved.is_relative_to(Path('configs').resolve()), f'Config path outside allowed directory: {resolved}'`. Alternatively, accept only a config name (not a full path) and join it onto a trusted base directory inside load_config(). This closes the traversal path regardless of how the caller is extended.

stage_handoff_notes: The attack surface of this pipeline is minimal: no auth, no network calls, no user-supplied data, no secrets in source, no PII. yaml.safe_load is correct — yaml.load() with an arbitrary Loader would be a finding, but safe_load restricts deserialization to safe YAML types only. configs/default.yaml contains no secrets. TabularDataset uses unseeded np.random — from a security lens this is a non-issue (random synthetic data, not a CSPRNG misuse concern). No hardcoded credentials, no tokens, no session or auth logic anywhere in scope. The one finding above is speculative-but-plausible path traversal — medium now but would become high immediately if cfg_path is ever wired to external input.
